focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 10

By the time Silas reached the last plank over the drainage cut, the night had gone so hot the boards sweated under his boots. Sump Ward lay low and shining under a skin of damp, every catwalk plank dark with old overflow, every wash line hanging slack as dead eels. The grow houses gave off their own weather. Warm green light leaked through the slats. Ferment steam rolled out sweet and rotten, apples left too long in a barrel, yeast, mud, the faint metallic bite of lamp chimneys cooling. He had come without a speech. He had left his hat at home, his mouth shut, and his good hand empty.

Aunt Vero waited at the west gate with a paper twist of fennel seed in one broad brown hand. She chewed it on long shifts because the digester steam turned her stomach mean, and the sweet anise smell sat oddly against the ward stink. Her white braid was looped up and pinned with a bent brass spoon. One eye tracked him cleanly. The other wandered toward the cut as if it had a better opinion of the evening.

"If you came to kneel," she said, "kneel where somebody can mop around you. If you came to lift, the west house is going pretty, and nobody here needs that shit."

Silas nodded once. "I came to lift."

"We'll see."

She let him through.

Inside, the heat thickened at once. The west house had two rows of shelf beds, a central table slick with cuttings, and a long ferment wall breathing through iron coils. The practical trays along the outer shelves should have been a workman's green, sturdy and ugly, good for kitchen flame and stair landings. Half of them had thrown a pale frill over the surface instead, a delicate gold-white lace that looked expensive from a distance and useless up close. The stuff crisped at the edges when it dried and spat too hot when burned. Fashion ha...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...s chest and one wet stocking squelching on the boards.

No one touched him on the way out. They did not need to. The crowd held open just enough space to make plain that it was not company. Behind him he heard bowls lifted again, benches dragged, somebody telling the children to keep back from the trough. The fiddler did not resume at once. Then, after ten paces, he did. The first notes came cautious and thin. He was checking whether the tune would hold.

Silas reached the last plank before the slope and turned despite himself.

The feast had already begun the work of closing without him. Aunt Vero was back at the tables, barking for more pepper mash. The knot-kneed fiddler found his reel again. The boy with the missing tooth leaned over the trough with two other children, pointing at Silas's lost boot and arguing some new ridiculous name for the shining slop around it. Inez had her back to him, one hand braced on the cider cask while she spoke to a young mother holding a drowsy child. She did not look up.

By the time he climbed into Lantern Steps, the mash on his clothes had started to cool and tighten. His right wrist throbbed with each heartbeat, thickening under the skin. The packet string slid down and tangled at his elbow. He tore it off with his left hand and almost flung it into the drainage cut. Instead he looked at the wet paper stuck together there, his own borrowed alphabet blurring green into gold in his fist.

He shoved the whole mess into his pocket.

From below, past the plank walks and grow-house roofs, the Feast of Mud Names climbed back into full sound: fiddle scrape, crockery knock, children around the trough shouting names over his lost boot until one of them caught and the ward answered with a fresh bark of laughter that followed him up the slope.
